title: Delivery / Operations Professional
audience: Service delivery, reliability, release and operations roles.

questions.text: What is the primary goal of "AIOps"?
options: To apply AI and Machine Learning to IT operations data to automate and improve operations.
To replace all human IT staff with AI for controlled production AI operations.
To develop new AI algorithms for operational tasks when managing incidents, releases, and users.
To exclusively manage cloud infrastructure as part of reliable operational governance.

questions.text: When an AI model is moved from development to actual use by customers, what phase is this commonly called?
options: Data annotation and labelling before a model is ready for use.
Deployment or Production.
Model training and evaluation before operational release.
Algorithm design before packaging the service for users.

questions.text: Why is "monitoring" an AI system in production important for a Delivery/Operations team?
options: To confirm the service is producing expected operational outputs, not unrelated content.
To solely manage its energy consumption as part of reliable operational governance within the same service management context.
To track its performance, detect failures, and identify degradation or unexpected behavior.
To see if the AI is chatting with users for controlled production AI operations.

questions.text: What does "model performance degradation" in an AI system typically refer to?
options: The deployed model package being corrupted during release or rollback activities.
The user interface becoming less usable while the underlying model remains stable.
A model responding slowly because of infrastructure capacity rather than prediction quality.
A decrease in the model's accuracy or effectiveness over time in a production environment.

questions.text: Which of these is a key responsibility for an operations professional when managing an AI-powered service?
options: Providing end-user technical support and incident management for the AI service.
Ensuring the AI model is regularly retrained with new data for controlled production AI operations.
Writing the underlying AI algorithms from scratch when managing incidents, releases, and users.
Designing the AI's internal architecture as part of reliable operational governance.

questions.text: What is an "SLA" (Service Level Agreement) in the context of an AI-driven service?
options: A specialised customer service model used to classify requests and responses for controlled production AI operations.
A contract defining the level of service expected from a provider, including uptime and performance.
A programming language used for writing AI models and integration code as part of reliable operational governance.
A feedback collection system for gathering user comments about AI services during live service delivery and support.

questions.text: In AI operational management, what is a "rollback" strategy primarily used for?
options: To add new behaviours to the model rather than restore a stable service version.
To automatically update the AI model daily as part of reliable operational governance.
To revert a deployed AI model to a previous stable version in case of issues.
To transfer AI data to a different server for controlled production AI operations.

questions.text: What is "model drift" and why is it a concern for operations teams?
options: When a model is migrated between servers without changing prediction quality.
A security vulnerability involving manipulated inputs that can cause incorrect outputs during live service delivery and support within the same service management context.
The tendency of AI models to slow down during peak usage for controlled production AI operations.
When the relationship between model inputs and the target output changes over time, causing decreased accuracy, requiring monitoring and potential retraining.

questions.text: When an AI system fails in production, what is "post-mortem analysis" intended to achieve?
options: To determine the root cause of the failure and prevent recurrence, improving incident response.
To celebrate the AI's eventual recovery for controlled production AI operations.
To redesign the entire AI system from scratch when managing incidents, releases, and users within the same service management context.
To officially decommission the AI system as part of reliable operational governance.

questions.text: What is a "feature flag" or "toggle" useful for in deploying AI features?
options: To mark interface elements connected to AI features without controlling rollout.
To enable or disable specific AI features or versions in a controlled manner, often for A/B testing or gradual rollout.
To retrain models on a schedule without release governance or impact testing.
To secure AI models from unauthorized access during live service delivery and support within the same service management context.

questions.text: In the context of AI operations, what is "dark launch" or "shadow mode" deployment?
options: Launching an AI model without telling anyone it's live when managing incidents, releases, and users within the same service management context.
Deploying a model during quiet periods solely to reduce operational disruption.
Deploying a new AI model in production alongside the existing one, running it on live data but not impacting user experience, to monitor its performance.
A security measure to hide the AI model's internal workings for controlled production AI operations.

questions.text: What role does "alerting" play in managing production AI systems?
options: To inform users about new AI features as part of reliable operational governance.
To schedule routine maintenance for AI hardware during live service delivery and support.
To warn the AI about potential cybersecurity threats for controlled production AI operations within the same service management context.
To proactively notify operations teams about anomalies, performance degradation, or errors in AI models.

questions.text: Why is "data pipeline monitoring" crucial for AI systems?
options: To detect issues with data quality, freshness, and throughput that could negatively impact AI model performance.
To ensure data is always stored in the most expensive cloud storage for controlled production AI operations within the same service management context.
To monitor the physical location of data centers when managing incidents, releases, and users.
To automatically generate new data for AI training as part of reliable operational governance.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of "automated testing" for AI features in a CI/CD pipeline?
options: It makes AI models more ethical for controlled production AI operations.
It accelerates deployment cycles and reduces manual effort by verifying AI functionality and performance consistently.
It creates new model code rather than coordinating releases and operational control.
It helps AI models understand human emotions better during live service delivery and support within the same service management context.

questions.text: Which of these best describes "Canary Releases" for AI models?
options: Releasing a new AI model to all users simultaneously when managing incidents, releases, and users.
Deploying an AI model only to users in specific geographical regions as part of reliable operational governance within the same service management context.
Gradually rolling out a new AI model to a small subset of users first, monitoring its performance, and then expanding the rollout if stable.
A method for training AI models faster than usual for controlled production AI operations.

questions.text: What is "A/B testing" commonly used for in AI development and operations?
options: To determine if an AI model can accurately predict two different outcomes as part of reliable operational governance within the same service management context.
To measure the energy consumption of AI systems during live service delivery and support.
To train two AI models simultaneously on the same dataset for controlled production AI operations.
To compare the performance of two different AI models or feature versions with live users to identify the superior one.

questions.text: In the context of ITIL or IT Service Management, what would be a key "problem management" activity related to a recurring AI model error?
options: Investigating the root cause of the failure and prevent recurrence, improving incident response.
Immediately restarting the AI system whenever an error occurs for controlled production AI operations.
Notifying all users about the AI error via email when managing incidents, releases, and users.
Redeploying the exact same AI model repeatedly as part of reliable operational governance.

questions.text: What is the significance of a "service level objective" (SLO) for an AI-powered API?
options: It's a marketing slogan for the AI service for controlled production AI operations.
It defines a target value for a service level indicator (SLI), specifying the desired performance or availability of the AI API.
It documents API structure but does not explain service-level user commitments.
It's a list of all features the AI API provides during live service delivery and support within the same service management context.

questions.text: What is "drift detection" in ML monitoring, beyond just model output drift?
options: Detecting whether model hosting infrastructure has been moved between environments.
Detecting unapproved model updates rather than shifts in real-world data patterns.
Monitoring changes in the input data distribution or target concept over time, which can indicate potential model degradation.
Detecting server performance metrics like CPU or memory spikes for controlled production AI operations within the same service management context.

questions.text: Which of these is a primary challenge in managing "AI incident response"?
options: AI outputs becoming too broad or creative for a controlled service workflow.
Deciding on a fun name for each AI incident during live service delivery and support.
The AI model being too fast for humans to keep up for controlled production AI operations within the same service management context.
Rapidly diagnosing and mitigating issues in complex, opaque AI systems, often requiring specialized ML expertise alongside traditional IT skills.
